Romulus and Remus.definition: The Roman practice of reading signs from the gods — especially the flight of birds — to determine the right course of action.
Romulus and Remus.context: Romulus and Remus settled their dispute by augury: watching for birds, and twelve vultures outweighed six.

Romulus and Remus.definition: Set apart as holy — belonging to the gods, not to be violated or treated as ordinary.
Romulus and Remus.context: The boundary of a Roman city was sacred; jumping over it, as Remus did, was an insult to the gods who protected it.

Romulus and Remus.definition: The person who is next in line to receive a title, throne, or inheritance.
Romulus and Remus.context: Amulius forced Rhea Silvia into the priesthood to prevent her from producing an heir who could challenge him.

The Founding of Rome.definition: The act of taking someone away by force — seizing a person against their will.
The Founding of Rome.context: The Romans acknowledged openly that the abduction of the Sabine women was not their finest moment, and told the story anyway.

The Founding of Rome.definition: To absorb and integrate people or ideas into a larger group, making them part of it.
The Founding of Rome.context: Rome's great strength was its ability to assimilate — to take in people from other cultures and make them Roman.

The Founding of Rome.definition: To step between two opposing sides and help them reach an agreement without violence.
The Founding of Rome.context: The Sabine women ran between the armies to mediate — they were the bridge, and neither side could fight through them.

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy.definition: In Roman terms: dutiful devotion to gods, family, and homeland — doing what you owe to those who have a claim on you.
Aeneas and the Fall of Troy.context: Aeneas carrying his father on his back through burning Troy was the Romans' defining image of piety.

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy.definition: A future that has been fixed or ordained — what must happen, regardless of personal preference.
Aeneas and the Fall of Troy.context: Aeneas's destiny was Italy and a new civilization; every delay and detour was only ever a delay, never a change of course.

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy.definition: A person forced to leave their home, usually due to disaster or danger, seeking safety elsewhere.
Aeneas and the Fall of Troy.context: Aeneas led a fleet of Trojan refugees — people with nothing left but the sacred objects they carried and the future they were promised.

Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey.definition: Certain to happen — impossible to prevent no matter what you do.
Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey.context: Juno could not stop what was inevitable, only delay it; the Fates had already decided Rome would rise from Troy's ashes.

Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey.definition: Active resistance or force working against you — someone or something blocking your progress.
Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey.context: Juno was Aeneas's constant divine opposition: storms, delays, the stay in Carthage — every obstacle was hers.

Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey.definition: Something you are obligated to do — an obligation that comes before personal preference.
Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey.context: When Mercury arrived with Jupiter's command, Aeneas left Dido because duty required it, even though the cost was real.

Jupiter, King of the Gods.definition: The right and power to make decisions and enforce them — legitimate power that others recognize and accept.
Jupiter, King of the Gods.context: Jupiter's authority was both divine and civic: not just king of the gods, but the divine guarantor of Roman law and order.

Jupiter, King of the Gods.definition: To call upon a god, spirit, or principle for help or witness — to formally name something as your support.
Jupiter, King of the Gods.context: Romans invoked Jupiter at the moment of oaths and treaties because his name made the promise binding.

Jupiter, King of the Gods.definition: Of or belonging to the gods — having a sacred, superhuman quality.
Jupiter, King of the Gods.context: Roman generals claimed their victories as Jupiter's, because all Roman success had divine backing behind it.

Juno, Queen of the Gods.definition: A female patron — a goddess or powerful woman who especially protects a particular group or institution.
Juno, Queen of the Gods.context: Juno was the patroness of Roman women, of marriage, and of the Roman state — three different domains, all equally fierce.

Juno, Queen of the Gods.definition: To restore a relationship after conflict — to come to peace with someone or something you have opposed.
Juno, Queen of the Gods.context: Juno reconciled with Rome in the end, because the Fates had always had her cornered; she simply refused to submit without a fight.

Juno, Queen of the Gods.definition: A calm, serious self-respect — the quality of someone who knows their own worth and maintains it under pressure.
Juno, Queen of the Gods.context: The Romans respected Juno's dignity: she fought the outcome she could not prevent, and she fought it magnificently.

Venus, Mother of Rome.definition: A line of descent — the chain of ancestors connecting a person to their origins.
Venus, Mother of Rome.context: The Julian family traced their lineage directly to Aeneas, and through Aeneas to Venus, making them literally divine in origin.

Venus, Mother of Rome.definition: A god or powerful person who especially protects and supports a particular place, group, or cause.
Venus, Mother of Rome.context: Venus was Rome's divine patron in the deepest sense: not just a helpful deity, but the literal ancestor of its founding family.

Venus, Mother of Rome.definition: Bringing culture, refinement, and the arts to people or places — the process of developing human society beyond bare survival.
Venus, Mother of Rome.context: Venus's role as patron of beauty justified the Romans' civilizing impulse: building magnificently was divine, not frivolous.

Mars, Father of Rome.definition: Controlled, orderly behavior maintained by training and habit — doing the right thing consistently, not just when you feel like it.
Mars, Father of Rome.context: Roman Mars represented military discipline: war as organized force, not chaos — the same quality that made Rome's armies unbeatable.

Mars, Father of Rome.definition: An organized military operation over a period of time — a planned series of military actions toward a goal.
Mars, Father of Rome.context: March was the start of the military campaign season, when roads thawed and armies could move again.

Mercury, the Messenger.definition: The buying and selling of goods and services — trade and business as a system.
Mercury, the Messenger.context: Mercury governed commerce because trade required the same quick judgment and reliable communication he embodied.

Mercury, the Messenger.definition: A guide of souls — a being whose role is to escort the dead on their journey to the afterlife.
Mercury, the Messenger.context: Mercury's role as psychopomp was tender: he walked with the dead as a guide walks with a nervous traveler, at their pace.

Mercury, the Messenger.definition: Mercury's winged staff wound with two serpents — a symbol of negotiation, safe passage, and later, medicine.
Mercury, the Messenger.context: A herald carrying the caduceus traveled under divine guarantee of safety; the staff meant: I come in peace, under Mercury's protection.

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization.definition: The ability to do something well — having the knowledge and skill to perform a task effectively.
Minerva and the Arts of Civilization.context: Minerva was the divine patron of competence: every craftsman, healer, teacher, and soldier who worked with skill worked under her protection.

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization.definition: A god or powerful person who especially protects and supports a particular group, craft, or cause.
Minerva and the Arts of Civilization.context: As patron of schools, Minerva's festival was when students sat their examinations and teachers made offerings at her shrines.

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization.definition: Controlled, trained skill — the ability that comes from sustained practice and careful learning.
Minerva and the Arts of Civilization.context: The gladiators in Minerva's festival were there because physical discipline belonged to her domain as much as intellectual discipline did.

Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman.definition: To change something completely from one form to another — especially to improve it or make it more useful.
Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman.context: Vulcan's domain was transformation: taking raw iron or bronze and making it into weapons, armor, tools — something with purpose.

Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman.definition: A workshop where metal is heated and shaped — or the act of making something through heat and force.
Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman.context: The volcanic islands off Sicily were, the Romans said, the vents of Vulcan's forge, built into the earth itself.

Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman.definition: The ability to recover from hardship and keep working — to be strengthened rather than broken by difficulty.
Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman.context: Vulcan's resilience was his defining quality: thrown from Olympus, injured, overlooked — and back at the forge, making the best things in creation.

Neptune and the Sea.definition: The area of power or responsibility that belongs to a particular god or ruler.
Neptune and the Sea.context: Neptune's domain was everything wet — sea, rivers, lakes, springs, and the earthquakes caused by water moving beneath the land.

Neptune and the Sea.definition: Permission or tolerance given by someone with power — being allowed to do something by someone who could stop you.
Neptune and the Sea.context: Every Roman ship crossed the sea on Neptune's sufferance — his permission, which he could revoke without warning.

Neptune and the Sea.definition: A three-pronged spear — Neptune's symbol of power, capable of splitting the earth or stilling the seas.
Neptune and the Sea.context: When Neptune drove his trident into the Acropolis rock, a spring appeared; it was the wrong gift, but it was real power.

Proserpina and the Pomegranate.definition: Tied or committed to something — obligated in a way you cannot escape.
Proserpina and the Pomegranate.context: The law of the underworld was clear: eat anything there and you are bound to it, six seeds or one seed or any number at all.

Proserpina and the Pomegranate.definition: Completely changed — made into something different, often something greater, by what you've experienced.
Proserpina and the Pomegranate.context: Proserpina went into the underworld as a girl and came out transformed into a queen — the seeds had bound her and made her.

Proserpina and the Pomegranate.definition: Supreme power and authority in your own domain — the right to rule and govern.
Proserpina and the Pomegranate.context: The Romans emphasized that Proserpina had real sovereignty in the underworld: she ruled it, she didn't just endure it.

Cupid and Psyche.definition: A severe test or trial — a difficult and painful experience that tests your limits.
Cupid and Psyche.context: Venus's four tasks were an ordeal: each one impossible on its face, each one requiring Psyche to go further than she thought she could.

Cupid and Psyche.definition: Living forever — exempt from death, or lasting beyond ordinary time.
Cupid and Psyche.context: Jupiter granted Psyche immortality at the end, making the marriage between love and soul permanent and divine.

Cupid and Psyche.definition: Continuing to work at something difficult without giving up — keeping going when stopping would be easier.
Cupid and Psyche.context: Psyche's defining quality was perseverance: four impossible tasks, and she attempted all four, weeping and moving forward anyway.

The Story of Lucretia.definition: Moral excellence — the quality of behaving according to the highest standards of right conduct.
The Story of Lucretia.context: The Romans defined Lucretia's virtue not just by what she did but by what she refused to let her death mean nothing.

The Story of Lucretia.definition: An earlier example that establishes a standard — something that future situations will be measured against.
The Story of Lucretia.context: Lucretia's final words were about precedent: she would not be used as an example that unchaste behavior was acceptable.

The Story of Lucretia.definition: A system of government where power is held by elected representatives rather than a single ruler.
The Story of Lucretia.context: Lucretia's death triggered the expulsion of the kings and the founding of the Roman Republic — the government that shaped Western civilization.

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law.definition: An ordinary Roman citizen — not a member of the patrician noble class.
The Twelve Tables and Roman Law.context: The plebeians went on strike and left the city because the law applied to them without being accessible to them.

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law.definition: Open and visible — not hidden or secret, available for anyone to see and examine.
The Twelve Tables and Roman Law.context: The Twelve Tables made Roman law transparent: put on bronze in the Forum, readable by any citizen.

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law.definition: To write rules or laws down in an organized, official form — to make informal customs into formal written law.
The Twelve Tables and Roman Law.context: The Decemviri's job was to codify Roman law: to take what had existed only in patrician memory and write it down for everyone.

Hercules: The Roman Hero.definition: In the Roman sense: moral excellence combined with active strength — not just being good, but doing good through effort and discipline.
Hercules: The Roman Hero.context: The Romans made Hercules a symbol of virtue: not power alone, but power directed by the choice to do the harder and better thing.

Hercules: The Roman Hero.definition: The elevation of a person to divine status — becoming a god after a mortal life of exceptional deeds.
Hercules: The Roman Hero.context: Hercules's apotheosis — his taking up to Olympus after death — was the Romans' proof that virtue had its ultimate reward.

Hercules: The Roman Hero.definition: Calm control of yourself under pressure — keeping your head when the situation is extreme.
Hercules: The Roman Hero.context: Holding up the sky, Hercules kept his composure: he thought, he acted, he walked away with the apples. That was the Roman lesson.
